html:
VII

Lilian and Vincent

The sun, already high in the heavens, caresses Vincent‚Äôs bare foot on the wide bed, where he is lying beside Lilian. She sits up and looks at him, not knowing that he is awake, and is astonished to see a look of anxiety on his face.

It is possible that Lady Griffith loved Vincent; but what she loved in him was success. Vincent was tall, handsome, slim, but he did not know how to hold himself, how to sit down or get up. He had an expressive face, but he did his hair badly. Above all she admired the boldness and robustness of his intellect; he was certainly highly educated, but she thought him uncultivated. With the instinct of a mistress and a mother, she hung over this big boy of hers and made it her task to form him. He was her creation‚ÅÝ‚Äîher statue. She taught him to polish his nails, to part his hair on one side instead of brushing it back, so that his brow, when it was half hidden by a stray lock, looked all the whiter and loftier. And then instead of the modest little ready-made bows he used to wear, she gave him really becoming neckties. Decidedly Lady Griffith loved Vincent; but she could not put up with him when he was silent or ‚Äúmoody,‚Äù as she called it.

She gently passes a finger over Vincent‚Äôs forehead, as though to efface a wrinkle‚ÅÝ‚Äîthose two deep vertical furrows which start from his eyebrows, and give his face a look almost of suffering.

‚ÄúIf you are going to bring me regrets, anxieties, remorse,‚Äù she murmurs, as she leans over him, ‚Äúit would be better never to come back.‚Äù

Vincent shuts his eyes as though to shut out too bright a light. The jubilation in Lilian‚Äôs face dazzles him.

‚ÄúYou must treat this as if it were a mosque‚ÅÝ‚Äîtake your shoes off before you come in, so as not to bring in any mud from the outside. Do you suppose I don‚Äôt know what you are thinking of?‚Äù Then, as Vincent tries to put his hand on her mouth, she defends herself with the grace of a naughty child.

‚ÄúNo! Let me speak to you seriously. I have reflected a great deal about what you said the other day. People always think that women aren‚Äôt capable of reflection, but you know, it depends upon the woman.‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ That thing you said the other day about the products of cross breeding‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ and that it isn‚Äôt by crossing that one gets satisfactory results so much as by selection.‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ Have I remembered your lesson, eh? Well, this morning I think you have bred a monster‚ÅÝ‚Äîa perfectly ridiculous creature‚ÅÝ‚Äîyou‚Äôll never rear it! A cross between a bacchante and the Holy Ghost! Haven‚Äôt you now?‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ You‚Äôre disgusted with yourself for having chucked Laura. I can tell it from the lines on your forehead. If you want to go back to her, say so at once and leave me; I shall have been mistaken in you and I shan‚Äôt mind in the least. But if you mean to stay with me, then get rid of that funereal countenance. You remind me of certain English people‚ÅÝ‚Äîthe more emancipated their opinions, the more they cling to their morality; so that there are no severer Puritans than their freethinkers.‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ You think I‚Äôm heartless? You‚Äôre wrong. I understand perfectly that you are sorry for Laura. But then, what are you doing here?‚Äù

Then, as Vincent turned his head away:

‚ÄúLook here! You must go to the bathroom now and try and wash your regrets off in the shower-bath. I shall ring for breakfast, eh? And when you come back, I‚Äôll explain something that you don‚Äôt seem to understand.‚Äù

He had got up. She sprang after him.

‚ÄúDon‚Äôt dress just yet. In the cupboard on the right hand side of the bath, you‚Äôll find a collection of burnouses and haiks and pyjamas. Take anything you like.‚Äù

Vincent appeared twenty minutes later dressed in a pistachio coloured silk jellabah.

‚ÄúOh, wait a minute‚ÅÝ‚Äîwait! Let me arrange you!‚Äù cried Lilian in delight. She pulled out of an oriental chest two wide purple scarves; wound the darker of the two as a sash round Vincent‚Äôs waist, and the other as a turban round his head.

‚ÄúMy thoughts are always the same colour as my clothes,‚Äù she said. (She had put on crimson and silver lam√© pyjamas.) ‚ÄúI remember once, when I was quite a little girl at San Francisco, I was put into black because a sister of my mother‚Äôs had died‚ÅÝ‚Äîan old aunt whom I had never seen. I cried the whole day long. I was terribly, terribly sad; I thought that I was very unhappy and that I was grieving deeply for my aunt‚Äôs death‚ÅÝ‚Äîall because I was in black. Nowadays, if men are more serious than women, it‚Äôs because their clothes are darker. I‚Äôll wager that your thoughts are quite different from what they were a little while ago. Sit down there on the bed; and when you‚Äôve drunk a glass of vodka and a cup of tea and eaten two or three sandwiches, I‚Äôll tell you a story. Say when I‚Äôm to begin.‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶‚Äù

She settled down on the rug beside the bed, crouching between Vincent‚Äôs legs like an Egyptian statue, with her chin resting on her knees. When she had eaten and drunk, she began:

‚ÄúI was on the Bourgogne, you know, on the day of the wreck. I was seventeen, so now you know how old I am. I was a very good swimmer, and to show you that I‚Äôm not hard-hearted, I‚Äôll tell you that if my first thought was to save myself, my second was to save someone else. I‚Äôm not quite sure even whether it wasn‚Äôt my first. Or rather, I don‚Äôt think I thought of anything; but nothing disgusts me so much in such moments as the people who only think of themselves‚ÅÝ‚Äîoh, yes‚ÅÝ‚Äîthe women who scream. There was a first boatload, chiefly of women and children, and some of them yelled to such an extent that it was enough to make anyone lose his head. The boat was so badly handled that instead of dropping down on to the sea straight, it dived nose foremost and everyone in it was flung out before it even had time to fill with water. The whole scene took place by the light of torches and lanterns and searchlights. You can‚Äôt imagine how ghastly it was. The waves were very big and everything that was not in the light was lost in darkness on the other side of the hill of water.

‚ÄúI have never lived more intensely; but I was as incapable of reflection as a Newfoundland dog, I suppose, when he jumps into the water. I can‚Äôt even understand now what happened; I only know that I had noticed a little girl in the boat‚ÅÝ‚Äîa darling thing of about five or six; and when I saw the boat overturn, I immediately made up my mind that it was her I would save. She was with her mother, but the poor woman was a bad swimmer; and as usual in such cases, her skirts hampered her. As for me, I expect I undressed mechanically; I was called to take my place in the second boatload. I must have got in; and then I no doubt jumped straight into the sea out of the boat; all I can remember is swimming about for a long time with the child clinging to my neck. It was terrified and clutched me so tight that I couldn‚Äôt breathe. Luckily the people in the boat saw us and either waited for us or rowed towards us. But that‚Äôs not why I‚Äôm telling you this story. The recollection which remains most vividly with me and which nothing will ever efface from my mind and my heart is this.‚ÅÝ‚ÄîThere were about forty or so of us in the boat, all crowded together, for a number of swimmers had been picked up at the last gasp like me. The water was almost on a level with the edge of the boat. I was in the stern and I was holding the little girl I had just saved tightly pressed against me to warm her‚ÅÝ‚Äîand to prevent her from seeing what I couldn‚Äôt help seeing myself‚ÅÝ‚Äîtwo sailors, one armed with a hatchet and the other with a kitchen chopper. And what do you think they were doing?‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ They were hacking off the fingers and hands of the swimmers who were trying to get into our boat. One of these two sailors (the other was a Negro) turned to me, as I sat there, my teeth chattering with cold and fright and horror, and said, ‚ÄòIf another single one gets in we shall be bloody well done for. The boat‚Äôs full.‚Äô And he added that it was a thing that had to be done in all shipwrecks, but that naturally one didn‚Äôt mention it.

‚ÄúI think I fainted then; at any rate, I can‚Äôt remember anything more, just as one remains deaf for a long time after a noise that has been too tremendous.

‚ÄúAnd when I came to myself on board the X, which picked us up, I realized that I was no longer the same, that I never could again be the same sentimental young girl I had been before; I realized that a part of myself had gone down with the Bourgogne; that henceforth there would be a whole heap of delicate feelings whose fingers and hands I should hack away to prevent them from climbing into my heart and wrecking it.‚Äù

She looked at Vincent out of the corner of her eye and, with a backward twist of her body, went on: ‚ÄúIt‚Äôs a habit one must get into.‚Äù

Then, as her hair, which she had pinned up loosely, was coming down and falling over her shoulders, she rose, went up to a mirror and began to rearrange it, talking as she did so:

‚ÄúWhen I left America a little later, I felt as if I were the golden fleece starting off in search of a conqueror. I may sometimes have been foolish‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ I may sometimes have made mistakes‚ÅÝ‚Äîperhaps I am making one now in talking to you like this‚ÅÝ‚Äîbut you, on your side, don‚Äôt imagine that because I have given myself to you, you have won me. Make certain of this‚ÅÝ‚ÄîI abominate mediocrity and I can love no one who isn‚Äôt a conqueror. If you want me, it must be to help you to victory; if it‚Äôs only to be pitied and consoled and made much of‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ no, my dear boy‚ÅÝ‚ÄîI‚Äôd better say so at once‚ÅÝ‚ÄîI‚Äôm not the person you need‚ÅÝ‚Äîit‚Äôs Laura.‚Äù

She said all this without turning round and while she was continuing to arrange her rebellious locks, but Vincent caught her eye in the glass.

‚ÄúMay I give you my answer this evening?‚Äù he said, getting up and taking off his oriental garments to get into his day clothes. ‚ÄúI must go home quickly now so as to catch my brother Olivier before he goes out. I‚Äôve got something to say to him.‚Äù

He said it by way of apology, to give colour to his departure; but when he went up to Lilian, she turned round to him smiling, and so lovely that he hesitated.

‚ÄúUnless I leave a line for him to get at lunch time,‚Äù he added.

‚ÄúDo you see a great deal of him?‚Äù

‚ÄúHardly anything. No, it‚Äôs an invitation for this afternoon, which I‚Äôve got to pass on to him.‚Äù

‚ÄúFrom Robert?‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ Oh! I see!‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶‚Äù2 she said, smiling oddly. ‚ÄúThat‚Äôs a person, too, I must talk to you about.‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ All right! Go at once. But come back at six o‚Äôclock, because at seven his car is coming to take us out to dinner in the Bois.‚Äù

Vincent walks home, meditating as he goes; he realizes that from the satisfaction of desire there may arise, accompanying joy and as it were sheltering behind it, something not unlike despair.
